---
title: "Interactive Statistics Demonstrations"
subtitle: "Understanding Sum of Squares, Variance, and Degrees of Freedom"
---

Welcome! These interactive modules are designed to help you build a visual, intuitive understanding of some of the most fundamental (and sometimes tricky) concepts in statistics.

Instead of just memorizing formulas, you are going to generate real data, watch how the math reacts, and see the geometric proofs of these concepts unfold in real-time.

### Choose a Module to Begin:

**[1. Single Sample Growth](single_sample.qmd)**

- **What you will do:** Draw individual scores one at a time from a population of your choice.
- **What you will learn:** Watch how the Sum of Squares ($SS$) grows as your sample size increases. More importantly, you will see exactly why calculating variance using the sample mean ($M$) systematically underestimates the true variance compared to using the population mean ($\mu$)—and why we have to divide by $n-1$ to fix it!

**[2. Sampling Distributions](sampling.qmd)**

- **What you will do:** Draw dozens (or hundreds!) of samples all at once.
- **What you will learn:** See the **Central Limit Theorem** in action as the distribution of sample means forms a bell curve. You will also track the average of all your sample variances to prove that dividing by $n-1$ creates an *unbiased estimator* that perfectly targets the true population variance ($\sigma^2$).

**[3. Independent Samples](ind_samples.qmd)**
- **What you will do:** Draw pairs of samples from the same population and compare them.
- **What you will learn:** Explore how the variability within each sample (SS₁ and SS₂) relates to the variability between the sample means (SS_between) and the variability of the grand mean (SS_grand). You will see how these concepts underpin the logic of ANOVA and why we use mean squares (MS) to estimate variance in this context.

**[4. Formulas Explained](formulas.qmd)**
- **What you will do:** Read through a detailed breakdown of the key formulas for Sum of Squares and Variance in the context of independent samples.
- **What you will learn:** Understand the algebraic structure of these formulas, how they relate to each other, and why they all converge on the true population variance in expectation.

**[5. Guess Correlation](guess_correlation.qmd)**
- **What you will do:** Adjust the number of points in a scatter plot and guess the correlation (r) before revealing the true value.
- **What you will learn:** Develop an intuitive sense for how correlation manifests in data, and how the strength of the relationship between two variables can be visually assessed.

::: {.callout-note}
These modules are designed to be exploratory and interactive. Feel free to experiment with different population parameters, sample sizes, and numbers of samples to see how the concepts hold up under various conditions. The goal is to develop a deep, intuitive understanding of these statistical principles, not just to see them work in one specific case.
:::
